I started cooking two years ago mostly out of guilt. I was spending $60 a day on delivery and couldn't name a single spice in my cabinet. The cabinet had three spices. One was salt.

The first few months were rough. I burned rice. Twice. I made a stir-fry so bad my smoke detector became a cooking timer. But somewhere around month four, I made a carbonara that actually tasted like carbonara, and something clicked. Not a dramatic revelation, just a quiet "oh, I can actually do this" moment.

What surprised me was how much it changed my relationship with time. Chopping onions is boring. Waiting for dough to rise is boring. But it's a different kind of boring than scrolling my phone, and I needed that. My brain gets to wander without any input for once.

The patience thing people talk about is real, though not in the inspirational-poster way. It's more that I've learned to read a recipe all the way through before starting, and that skill has leaked into other parts of my life. I read instructions for IKEA furniture now. Growth.

Cooking for other people is complicated. When it goes well, it's maybe the best feeling. My mom cried over a soup I made from her old recipe, and I still think about that. When it goes badly, you've wasted everyone's evening and there's nothing to eat. I've served inedible food to guests and had to order pizza at 9pm. You just have to laugh about it.

I won't pretend I'm good at this. I have maybe eight dishes I can make reliably. But I eat better, I spend less, and once in a while I make something that surprises me.
